disqualified_students.authors: Leonard K. M. Poon, Nevin L. Zhang, Tao Chen, Yi Wang
disqualified_students.paper_title: Variable Selection in Model-Based Clustering: To Do or To Facilitate
disqualified_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
disqualified_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it proposes a methodological extension of Gaussian Mixture Models to handle variable relevance in high-dimensional clustering. It is a classical, EM-based probabilistic model with clear theoretical grounding and reproducible experiments on synthetic or public datasets using CPU-only computation.

disqualified_students.primary_method: ARIMA / Time Series, Support Vector Machine (SVM)
disqualified_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
disqualified_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it proposes a novel kernel method (Global Alignment Kernel) that makes SVM directly applicable to time series classification. The core contribution is methodological — it proves positive definiteness of the kernel, which is theoretically rigorous. The UCR Time Series dataset is publicly available and experiments are reproducible on a CPU-only laptop using tslearn and scikit-learn.

valid_students.authors: Tomoharu Iwata, Takeshi Yamada, Yasushi Sakurai, Naonori Ueda
valid_students.paper_title: Online Multiscale Dynamic Topic Models
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because its primary contribution — the Online Multiscale Dynamic Topic Model (MDTM) — directly aligns with my project on Dynamic Trend and Event Detection from text streams. The paper introduces a novel GMM/EM-based probabilistic topic model that tracks how topics evolve over time at multiple timescales, which is exactly the core problem my project addresses. It is published in KDD 2010 (CORE A*), introduces a clear methodological contribution, and can be reproduced on publicly available text datasets using CPU-only classical ML tools. The paper also includes multiple LDA-based baselines, making experimental comparison straightforward for Part B.

valid_students.authors: Brian Kulis, Michael I. Jordan
valid_students.paper_title: Revisiting k-means: New Algorithms via Bayesian Nonparametrics
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it sits at the intersection of classical GMM/EM theory and Bayesian nonparametrics. The core idea — that DP-means is the limiting case of a Dirichlet Process GMM — is elegant, mathematically grounded, and directly builds on the EM framework taught in class. The paper is reproducible using only numpy and scikit-learn on any standard clustering dataset such as Iris or synthetic blobs, with no GPU or proprietary data required. Additionally, the paper remains actively cited in modern research for automatic cluster number selection, making it both historically significant and practically relevant.

valid_students.authors: Sida Wang & Christopher D. Manning
valid_students.paper_title: Baselines and Bigrams: Simple, Good Sentiment and Topic Classification
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this

valid_students.authors: Lexiang Ye, Eamonn Keogh
valid_students.paper_title: Time Series Shapelets: A New Primitive for Data Mining
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose **Time Series Shapelets: A New Primitive for Data Mining** because it introduces shapelets as an intuitive and interpretable method for time-series classification, which fits the classical modeling category allowed in the exam. The math is simple (mainly distance calculations and information gain), and it uses small public **UCR Time Series Classification Archive** datasets that are reproducible and CPU-friendly. The approach is visual and easy to explain (plotting shapelets on series), making assumptions, limitations, and failure cases clearer. Compared to SVM or GMM papers, it is much simpler to reimplement and understand deeply.

valid_students.authors: Pinar Donmez, Jaime G. Carbonell, Jeff Schneider
valid_students.paper_title: Efficiently Learning the Accuracy of Labeling Sources for Selective Sampling
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it proposes a clear methodological contribution for learning from multiple noisy labeling sources in an active learning setting. The IEThresh algorithm introduces a principled exploration–exploitation tradeoff using confidence interval estimation. The method is computationally lightweight, reproducible on standard benchmark datasets such as UCI, and feasible to implement on a CPU-only setup.

valid_students.authors: Daniel Zoran and Yair Weiss
valid_students.paper_title: From Natural Images to GMMs and Dead Leaves
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this NeurIPS 2012 (CORE A)* paper because it provides a rigorous methodological advancement in Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) via the EPLL framework, aligning perfectly with the Unit 3 syllabus. The algorithm is strictly classical, avoiding deep learning, and is highly feasible for CPU-only reproduction on a MacBook through efficient eigendecomposition. Furthermore, it offers clear, observable failure modes for the diagnostic and residual analysis required in Parts B and C

valid_students.authors: Michele Cossalter, Rong Yan, Lu Zheng
valid_students.paper_title: Adaptive Kernel Approximation for Large-Scale Non-Linear SVM Prediction
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it proposes a methodological improvement to classical nonlinear Support Vector Machines by introducing an adaptive kernel approximation technique for efficient large-scale prediction. Since SVMs and kernel methods are a core component of the syllabus (Unit 2), this paper aligns directly with course content. The method is clearly described, experimentally validated, and can be reasonably reproduced using publicly available datasets on a CPU-only setup, making it suitable for analytical and empirical evaluation in Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Tomasz Malisiewicz, Abhinav Gupta, Alexei A. Efros
valid_students.paper_title: Ensemble of Exemplar-SVMs for Object Detection and Beyond
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this 2011 ICCV paper because it introduces a simple and powerful classical SVM variant (Exemplar-SVM ensemble) with almost zero heavy mathematics — only standard linear SVM training that I can implement directly with scikit-learn LinearSVC. The core idea is intuitive (one SVM per training exemplar + ensemble scoring), and all results are highly visual and materialistic: success/failure can be clearly shown with images (occlusion, viewpoint change, pose, lighting). It uses the public PASCAL VOC dataset (small subsets run in minutes on a normal laptop, CPU-only). This perfectly satisfies all Part A constraints (2009–2012, CORE A* venue, methodological contribution, easy reproduction) and will allow me to do strong visual experiments and clear reasoning in Parts B and C without struggling with complex equations.

valid_students.authors: Florent Perronnin, Jorge Sánchez, Thomas Mensink
valid_students.primary_method: Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM), Support Vector Machine (SVM)
valid_students.paper_title: Improving the Fisher Kernel for Large-Scale Image Classification
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: This paper seamlessly bridges two fundamental classical machine learning algorithms—Gaussian Mixture Models and Support Vector Machines—creating an incredibly robust computer vision pipeline. It aligns perfectly with my ongoing work on extracting classical visual features (like HOG and LBP) for image classification tasks without relying on deep neural networks. Furthermore, the methodology relies on closed-form mathematical steps that are computationally feasible to reproduce on a standard CPU using a simplified toy subset of the PASCAL VOC dataset.

valid_students.authors: Iftekhar Naim  , Daniel Gildea
valid_students.paper_title: Convergence of the EM Algorithm for Gaussian Mixtures with Unbalanced Mixing Coefficients  (2012)
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it provides a rigorous theoretical analysis of the EM algorithm for Gaussian Mixture Models, specifically addressing convergence behavior under unbalanced mixing coefficients. Since GMM and EM are core components of the syllabus (Unit 3), this paper aligns directly with course content while offering deeper insight into convergence dynamics and likelihood geometry. The paper allows controlled experimental reproduction using synthetic datasets, making it suitable for analytical and empirical study.

valid_students.authors: Chia-Hua Ho, Chih-Jen Lin
valid_students.paper_title: Large-scale Linear Support Vector Regression
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because its primary contribution is methodological , focusing on scaling up linear Support Vector Regression (an SVM variant). It strictly adheres to the exam constraints, being published in a CORE A* venue (JMLR) within the required 2009-2012 timeframe. Most importantly, it is highly feasible to reproduce for Parts B and C ; the linear nature of the algorithm ensures it can run efficiently on a standard CPU-only laptop without GPU dependencies , and it can be effectively evaluated using publicly available, tabular toy datasets.

valid_students.authors: Battista Biggio, Blaine Nelson, Pavel Laskov
valid_students.paper_title: Poisoning Attacks against Support Vector Machines
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it’s a classic ICML framework that bridges the gap between high-level math and real-world security. It’s rare to find a top-tier paper that is this impactful yet simple enough to reproduce in 50 lines of Python on a standard laptop.

valid_students.authors: Stefan Rueping
valid_students.paper_title: SVM Classifier Estimation from Group Probabilities
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose “SVM Classifier Estimation from Group Probabilities” by Stefan Rueping (ICML 2010) because it is a clean and easy-to-understand methodological variant of classical Support Vector Machines. It introduces a simple “inverse calibration” idea (very similar to Platt scaling) that estimates an SVM classifier directly from group probabilities instead of individual labels. The paper has clear diagrams in Figure 1 and Figure 2 that visually explain the problem and solution, making it perfect for explaining in Part C. It uses small public UCI datasets (2+ continuous numeric features) and can be reproduced on a standard laptop using scikit-learn SVR with toy 2D data or simplified code in under 100 lines. No GPU, no proprietary data, no heavy compute. It fully satisfies every rule in Part A (2009–2012, CORE A* venue, methodological SVM contribution, CPU-only). This choice will help me score high in reproducibility and allow clear discussion of failure cases (small groups) and inference in later parts.

valid_students.authors: Vojtěch Franc, Alexander Zien, Bernhard Schölkopf
valid_students.paper_title: Support Vector Machines as Probabilistic Models
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: among all possible SVM/GMM/ARIMA papers from that era, this one has the lowest risk and highest feasibility while still being a genuine, high-quality classical ML contribution from top authors (Schölkopf et al.). It’s the kind of paper that lets you focus on understanding and explaining rather than fighting with data or compute issues.

valid_students.authors: Yin-Wen Chang, Cho-Jui Hsieh, Kai-Wei Chang, Michael Ringgaard, Chih-Jen Lin
valid_students.paper_title: Training and Testing Low-degree Polynomial Data via Linear SVM
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it provides a highly practical methodological alternative to standard kernel SVMs. By explicitly mapping data to low-degree polynomials and applying a linear solver, it avoids the heavy computational bottlenecks of the traditional kernel trick. This ensures my Part B experiments will be highly feasible on a standard CPU, while giving me clear computational trade-offs to analyze in Part C.

valid_students.authors: Abdullah Mueen, Eamonn Keogh, Qiang Zhu, Sydney Cash, Brandon Westover
valid_students.paper_title: Exact Discovery of Time Series Motifs
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it introduces a foundational methodological advancement in the exact discovery of time series motifs using the MK algorithm. The method is highly interpretable, relying on classical distance metrics and the triangle inequality to prune search space rather than "black-box" approximations. It is computationally efficient enough to be reproduced on a standard personal laptop using publicly available UCR time-series datasets. The visual nature of motifs (repeating patterns) allows for clear, grounded reasoning during proctored evaluation, making it ideal for the requirements of Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Zhu et al
valid_students.paper_title: Learning Sparse SVM for Feature Selection on Very High Dimensional Data
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected “Learning Sparse SVM for Feature Selection on Very High Dimensional Data” (ICML 2010) because it extends classical SVMs with an embedded feature-selection mechanism, addressing the practical challenge of learning in high-dimensional settings. The paper introduces a sparse SVM formulation using feature-level regularization, enabling simultaneous classification and interpretability within a single optimization framework. Its experiments are reproducible on CPU-based systems using standard high-dimensional datasets and linear SVM implementations with sparsity constraints. The method allows clear analysis of trade-offs between sparsity, accuracy, and regularization, making it both experimentally feasible and conceptually strong for detailed discussion in Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Ľubor Ladický, Philip H.S. Torr
valid_students.paper_title: Locally Linear Support Vector Machines
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it presents a clear methodological extension of standard SVMs using locally linear classifiers, making it conceptually grounded in classical ML. The core idea — approximating a non-linear decision boundary using locally linear regions — is intuitive and builds directly on standard SVM theory. The datasets used (MNIST, USPS, Letter) are freely and publicly available.

valid_students.authors: Jialei Wang, Peilin Zhao, Steven C.H. Hoi
valid_students.paper_title: Exact Soft Confidence-Weighted Learning
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected "Exact Soft Confidence-Weighted Learning" (Wang, Zhao & Hoi, ICML 2012) because it introduces a novel, self-contained algorithm that extends online SVM-style learning by combining soft-margin handling with confidence-weighted updates — a clear methodological contribution. Published at ICML 2012 (A* venue), it requires no deep learning, no restricted data, and is fully reproducible on public datasets with minimal compute, making it straightforward to understand and explain end-to-end.

valid_students.authors: Zhuang Wang , Koby Crammer , Slobodan Vucetic
valid_students.paper_title: Breaking the Curse of Kernelization: Budgeted Stochastic Gradient Descent for Large-Scale SVM Training
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected “Breaking the Curse of Kernelization: Budgeted Stochastic Gradient Descent for Large-Scale SVM Training” (JMLR 2012) because it presents a clear methodological improvement to a core classical machine learning algorithm, the Support Vector Machine. The paper introduces the Budgeted Stochastic Gradient Descent (BSGD) approach, which controls the number of support vectors and makes kernel SVM training scalable while preserving predictive performance. The method directly aligns with the SVM component of the syllabus and has well-defined objective functions, assumptions, and failure cases that can be analyzed. The algorithm can be reproduced on a standard CPU using synthetic or small public datasets, and experiments such as decision boundary visualization, effect of regularization, and impact of budget size can be demonstrated clearly. This makes the paper both theoretically meaningful and practically feasible for implementation and explanation in later parts of the examination.

valid_students.authors: "Abdullah Mueen", "Eamonn Keogh", "Neal Young"
valid_students.paper_title: Logical-Shapelets: An Expressive Primitive for Time Series Classification
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected Logical-Shapelets: An Expressive Primitive for Time Series Classification (Mueen, Keogh, Young — KDD 2011) because it introduces a genuinely new algorithmic primitive: logical combinations (AND/OR) of shapelets for time series classification. This extends the original shapelet framework — a distinct prior contribution by different authors at KDD 2009 — with two entirely new algorithms: sufficient statistics enabling amortized O(1) distance computation, and admissible pruning that provably skips unpromising candidates. KDD is CORE A*, the year 2011 falls within the required window, and the paper is methodological with a formal admissibility proof. Experiments use UCR benchmark datasets freely available online. The algorithm requires only NumPy-level operations and runs in minutes on a CPU laptop using the GunPoint dataset (200 time series, length 150). The paper is exactly 9 pages with explicit pseudocode, making the algorithm straightforward to understand and implement independently.

valid_students.authors: Mehmet Gönen, Ethem Alpaydın
valid_students.paper_title: Multiple Kernel Learning Algorithms
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it provides a comprehensive methodological framework for Multiple Kernel Learning (MKL). Instead of relying on a single kernel, it demonstrates how to optimize a linear combination of base kernels during SVM training. The alternating optimization strategy (updating SVM alphas and then kernel weights) is mathematically elegant, structurally simple to code from scratch, and highly feasible to reproduce on my standard CPU using a basic toy classification dataset.

valid_students.authors: Lijun Zhang, Rong Jin, Chun Chen, Jiajun Bu, Xiaofei He
valid_students.paper_title: Efficient Online Learning for Large-Scale Sparse Kernel Logistic Regression
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected Efficient Online Learning for Large-Scale Sparse Kernel Logistic Regression (AAAI 2012) because it provides a clear methodological improvement to kernel-based classification within the SVM/kernel methods framework. The approach is conceptually well-defined, aligns with course topics such as regularization and kernel representations, and can be reproduced on a standard CPU setup using simple datasets. It therefore offers a suitable balance of theoretical relevance and practical feasibility for further analysis in later parts of the examination.

valid_students.authors: Hamed Masnadi-Shirazi and Nuno Vasconcelos
valid_students.paper_title: Risk minimization, probability elicitation, and cost-sensitive SVMs
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it introduces a clear, methodological modification to the standard SVM hinge loss for handling imbalanced data. The concept of cost-sensitive learning via probability elicitation is mathematically intuitive, which will allow me to confidently explain the objective function without relying on tools later. Additionally, it is completely feasible to reproduce the custom loss function from scratch and test it on small, publicly available tabular datasets using just a CPU

valid_students.authors: Leonard K. M. Poon, Nevin L. Zhang, Tao Chen, Yi Wang
valid_students.paper_title: Variable Selection in Model-Based Clustering: To Do or To Facilitate
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it proposes a methodological extension of Gaussian Mixture Models to handle variable relevance in high-dimensional clustering. It is a classical, EM-based probabilistic model with clear theoretical grounding and reproducible experiments on synthetic or public datasets using CPU-only computation.

valid_students.authors: Andrea Vedaldi and Andrew Zisserman
valid_students.paper_title: Efficient Additive Kernels via Explicit Feature Maps
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it has a clear methodological contribution — deriving explicit feature maps for additive homogeneous kernels (chi-squared, intersection, Hellinger's) that allow nonlinear SVMs to be replaced by fast linear ones with provable approximation error bounds. The method is directly implemented in scikit-learn as AdditiveChi2Sampler, making full reproduction possible on a CPU-only laptop in under 2 seconds using the built-in load_digits dataset. The venue (TPAMI) is confirmed CORE A* under ERA2010 (Portal ID: 168). The inference procedure is explicit and mathematically clean — a feature map followed by a single dot product — which I can explain and implement from scratch without any tools. I also verified the key assumptions and failure modes through independent testing, including confirming the silent NaN failure on negative inputs.

valid_students.authors: Shai Shalev-Shwartz; Yoram Singer; Nathan Srebro; Andrew Cotter
valid_students.paper_title: Pegasos: Primal Estimated sub-GrAdient SOlver for SVM
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it introduces Pegasos, an efficient stochastic sub-gradient method for training Support Vector Machines in the primal form. The work provides strong theoretical guarantees along with practical scalability for large datasets. The algorithm is computationally efficient, reproducible on standard CPU hardware, and suitable for systematic experimental analysis. It also enables clear study of convergence behavior, regularization effects, and comparison with classical SVM solvers, making it well-suited for in-depth evaluation in Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Roi Livni, Koby Crammer, Amir Globerson
valid_students.paper_title: A Simple Geometric Interpretation of SVM using Stochastic Adversaries
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it provides a theoretically grounded reinterpretation of Support Vector Machines through a stochastic adversarial framework. Rather than proposing a new algorithm, it deepens understanding of classical SVM by linking it to a minimax geometric perspective. This aligns strongly with the objective of mastering a core classical method. The paper is concise, mathematically structured, and feasible to analyze and reproduce using standard SVM implementations on a CPU-only setup. Additionally, it is published in AISTATS 2012, a CORE A* venue within the required year range.

valid_students.authors: Anthony Bagnall, Jason Lines, Aaron Bostrom, James Large, Eamonn Keogh
valid_students.paper_title: Time Series Classification with COTE: The Collective of Transformation-Based Ensembles
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it proposes a clear and reproducible method for time-series classification using transformation-based ensembles. It uses publicly available UCR datasets, requires only CPU computation, and aligns directly with the Time Series unit of the syllabus, making it suitable for implementation and explanation in later parts.

valid_students.authors: Andrew Cotter, Shai Shalev-Shwartz, Nathan Srebro
valid_students.paper_title: The Kernelized Stochastic Batch Perceptron
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it proposes a novel and theoretically strong optimization algorithm (SBP) for training kernelized SVMs with improved runtime guarantees. It provides clear mathematical analysis, comparative experiments on public datasets, and is practical to reproduce while offering strong scope for evaluation.

valid_students.authors: Hsuan-Tien Lin, Ling Li
valid_students.paper_title: One-Sided Support Vector Regression for Multiclass Cost-Sensitive Classification
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it is a direct and clear methodological  extension of classical Support Vector Machines, modifying the standard  hinge loss to incorporate asymmetric misclassification costs for  multiclass problems.

valid_students.authors: Myle Ott, Yejin Choi, Claire Cardie, Jeffrey T. Hancock
valid_students.paper_title: Finding Deceptive Opinion Spam by Any Stretch of the Imagination
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it satisfies every exam constraint while being the most feasible option for reproduction. The primary contribution is entirely SVM-based — the paper proposes and compares three SVM classifiers (unigram n-gram SVM, LIWC psycholinguistic SVM, and POS-tag SVM) to detect fake hotel reviews, making SVM the core method and not just a baseline. It is published in ACL 2011, a CORE A* venue explicitly listed in the exam instructions. The exact original dataset (1,600 labeled hotel reviews) is freely downloadable from the author's own page at myleott.com with no login or approval required. The entire pipeline — TF-IDF vectorization plus LinearSVC plus 5-fold cross-validation — can be reproduced in under 25 lines of Python using only scikit-learn, running in seconds on a CPU-only laptop. The concept is also intuitive enough to explain without tools in Part C: fake reviews use imaginative and emotional language while genuine reviews use specific factual details, and a linear SVM learns a hyperplane in word-frequency space that separates these two writing styles. I verified the venue ranking on the CORE portal, confirmed the dataset downloads correctly, and checked the live response sheet to ensure no classmate had already selected this paper.

valid_students.authors: Marco Cuturi
valid_students.paper_title: Fast Global Alignment Kernels
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I came across this paper while exploring the intersection of time series and kernel methods, and it immediately stood out to me. The core idea — that Dynamic Time Warping, which is typically just a distance measure, can be reformulated as a proper positive-definite kernel — felt both surprising and elegant. This opens the door to using it directly with SVMs, which makes it a genuinely methodological contribution rather than just an application of existing tools. What drew me to it further was the balance it strikes: the math is rigorous enough to be interesting, but the experiments are reproducible using the UCR Time Series Archive, which is publicly available and well-known. I don't need special hardware, proprietary data, or complex infrastructure to work with it. It's from ICML 2011, clearly a top-tier venue, and it's focused on a classical method — no deep learning involved. It felt like the kind of paper where I could genuinely understand what's happening at every step, which matters a lot given that I'll need to explain my work in Part C.

valid_students.authors: Pedro Felzenszwalb, Ross Girshick, David McAllester, Deva Ramanan
valid_students.paper_title: Object Detection with Discriminatively Trained Part-Based Models
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it presents a clear methodological contribution using latent SVMs for object detection within the classical machine learning era. The experimental pipeline is well-defined and reproducible on a CPU-only setup using publicly available datasets such as PASCAL VOC or suitable smaller substitutes. The paper also provides interpretable failure cases and a manageable implementation scope, making it appropriate for detailed analysis and reproduction in Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Antoine Bordes, Léon Bottou, Patrick Gallinari
valid_students.paper_title: SGD-QN: Careful Quasi-Newton Stochastic Gradient Descent
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it introduces SGD-QN, a novel SVM optimization algorithm that incorporates second-order quasi-Newton curvature information into stochastic gradient descent, published in JMLR 2009, a CORE A★ venue. The core contribution is purely methodological — it proposes a faster, smarter training algorithm for linear SVMs without significantly increasing per-iteration cost. Official open-source code is provided by the authors, and experiments can be reproduced on publicly available datasets on a standard CPU-only laptop. The paper enables clear analysis of convergence speed trade-offs between first-order SGD methods like Pegasos and second-order approaches, and has well-defined failure modes on dense or ill-conditioned data — making it ideal for deep reasoning and experimentation in Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Pedro Felzenszwalb & Deva Ramanan
valid_students.paper_title: Object Detection with Grammar Models
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it introduces a structured approach to object detection using grammar-based models.

valid_students.authors: Ognjen Arandjelovic Roberto Cipolla
valid_students.paper_title: Incremental Learning of Temporally-Coherent Gaussian Mixture Models
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it presents a clear methodological extension of classical Gaussian Mixture Models by introducing incremental learning for temporally coherent data. The work builds directly on the EM algorithm and provides a practical solution for streaming or evolving datasets. The method is computationally feasible on a CPU-only setup and can be reproduced using synthetic or publicly available datasets. Additionally, the paper enables meaningful experimental analysis, such as studying convergence behavior, parameter stability, adaptation speed, and sensitivity to learning rates, which aligns well with the objectives of Parts B and C of the examination

valid_students.authors: Carl Brunner, Andreas Fischer, Klaus Luig, Thorsten Thies
valid_students.paper_title: Pairwise Support Vector Machines and their Application to Large Scale Problems.
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it extends a concept we studied in class, SVM, in a new and intresting way. The idea of predicting relationships between pairs of data points felt kind of unique and very practical. I also liked that the paper balances theory and application, so I could both understand the concepts and try implementing them myself.

valid_students.authors: Sören Sonnenburg and Vojtěch Franc
valid_students.paper_title: COFFIN: A Computational Framework for Linear SVMs
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: The reason why i choose this paper is because it proposes a clear and practical methodological improvement for training linear Support Vector Machines efficiently on large-scale datasets. The approach is reproducible using small public or synthetic datasets, runs on CPU-only systems, and allows experiments that illustrate optimization behavior and scalability.

valid_students.authors: Ingo Steinwart, Don Hush, Clint Scovel
valid_students.paper_title: Training SVMs Without Offset
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it makes a core ML idea much simpler. By removing the bias term from SVM, the math becomes easier, training becomes faster, and accuracy stays the same. I liked how clear and intuitive it is, which makes it easier for me to implement and explain.

valid_students.authors: Yin-Wen Chang, Cho-Jui Hsieh, Kai-Wei Chang, Michael Ringgaard, Chih-Jen Lin
valid_students.paper_title: Training and Testing Low-degree Polynomial Data Mappings via Linear SVM
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it presents a practical approach to scaling SVM for nonlinear problems by combining polynomial feature mapping with linear SVM. The connection to LIBLINEAR (widely used in production) and the clear trade-off between feature explosion and model expressiveness made it interesting. The paper's high citations (500+) also indicates its influence in the field.

valid_students.authors: Seyda Ertekin, Léon Bottou, and C. Lee Giles
valid_students.paper_title: Nonconvex Online Support Vector Machines
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: It seemed really interesting, it focuses on mathematical aspect to vercome the shortcomings

valid_students.authors: John Duchi Elad Hazan Yoram Singer
valid_students.paper_title: Adaptive Subgradient Methods for Online Learning and Stochastic Optimization
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it introduces AdaGrad, a simple and powerful method that improves how models learn. Instead of using a fixed learning rate, AdaGrad automatically adjusts the learning rate during training, which makes training more stable and efficient. It works well for common machine learning models like SVM and logistic regression. The method is practical, easy to implement, and can be tested on normal datasets,

valid_students.authors: Nicola Segata, Enrico Blanzieri
valid_students.paper_title: Fast and Scalable Local Kernel Machines
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it proposes a fast and scalable variant of kernel Support Vector Machines using local neighborhoods, making SVM training more efficient without sacrificing accuracy. It is a strong methodological contribution published in JMLR, and its results can be reproduced using standard datasets without heavy computational requirements.

valid_students.authors: Gui-Bo Ye, Yifei Chen, Xiaohui Xie
valid_students.paper_title: Efficient variable selection in support vector machines via the alternating direction method of multipliers
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it proposes a clear methodological improvement to classical Support Vector Machines using the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) for solving L1-regularized SVM efficiently. The paper is published in ICML 2011 (CORE A*) and strictly satisfies the 2009–2012 requirement mentioned in the exam guidelines.  The work is fully within classical machine learning and does not involve any deep learning techniques. The optimization formulation is clearly explained in the paper, which makes it easier to understand and reproduce on a standard CPU-based personal laptop. The experiments can be replicated using publicly available datasets such as UCI datasets or other standard benchmark datasets.  From a research perspective, this paper is interesting because it combines convex optimization with feature selection in SVM, which is both theoretically meaningful and practically useful. At the same time, the algorithmic steps are structured and implementable at a student level, making it a good balance between research depth and reproducibility for Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Andrea Vedaldi, Varun Gulshan, Manik Varma, Andrew Zisserman
valid_students.paper_title: Multiple Kernels for Object Detection
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: because this paper complies all the guidelines that were mentioned in the guidelines and was easier among all papers that I found so far

valid_students.authors: Zhenxing Xing, Jian Pei, Eamonn Keogh
valid_students.paper_title: Early Prediction on Time Series: A Nearest Neighbor Approach
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this IJCAI 2009 (CORE A*) paper because it presents a clear methodological advancement in time-series classification through an early prediction framework that enables accurate labeling using only partial observations of a sequence. The approach extends classical nearest-neighbor models with a principled earliness–accuracy trade-off mechanism, aligning well with the classical time-series learning concepts covered in the syllabus. The method is strictly non-deep-learning and is highly feasible for CPU-only reproduction on a standard laptop using publicly available time-series datasets. Furthermore, the model exhibits intuitive and observable behaviors under varying prefix lengths, supporting the experimental evaluation and error analysis required in Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Georgios Paltoglou, Mike Thelwall
valid_students.paper_title: A Study of Information Retrieval Weighting Schemes for Sentiment Analysis
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected "A Study of Information Retrieval Weighting Schemes for Sentiment Analysis" (Paltoglou & Thelwall, ACL 2010) because it provides a clean, direct methodological contribution based on Support Vector Machines, published in ACL 2010 — a CORE A* venue explicitly listed in the exam guidelines. The paper systematically investigates how different tf-idf and IR-style term weighting schemes (binary, raw tf, tf-idf, delta-tf-idf, BM25 variants) affect the accuracy of a linear SVM classifier on sentiment classification tasks. This fits squarely in the SVM/kernel methods category. The datasets used — the Movie Review corpus and the Multi-Domain Sentiment Dataset — are standard, freely available NLP benchmarks requiring no special access or approvals. The entire pipeline (bag-of-words vectorization + LinearSVC + cross-validation) is reproducible in under 40 lines of Python using scikit-learn on a CPU-only laptop in seconds. There is no dependency parsing, no graph optimization, and no custom solvers required. The core idea — that the choice of term weighting scheme significantly impacts SVM sentiment accuracy — is intuitive and easy to explain without tools in Part C. I verified the venue on the CORE portal, confirmed the datasets are publicly downloadable, and checked the live response sheet to ensure no classmate has selected this paper.

valid_students.authors: Lei Li, James McCann, Nancy Pollard,
valid_students.primary_method: ARIMA / Time Series, Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)
valid_students.paper_title: DynaMMo: Mining and Summarization of Coevolving Sequences with Missing Values
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: The paper addresses a genuinely important and ubiquitous problem — real-world time series data is almost never complete. Sensors fail, network packets drop, measurements get corrupted. Every domain from healthcare to environmental monitoring to finance suffers from missing values in sequential data. DynaMMo directly solves this by asking: "Can we recover what was missing by learning the hidden structure driving co-evolving sequences?" This motivation is immediately intuitive and easy to justify to any examiner.

valid_students.authors: Hsiang-Fu Yu, Fang-Lan Huang, Chih-Jen Lin
valid_students.paper_title: Dual coordinate descent methods for logistic regression and maximum entropy models
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it offers a clear methodological improvement on a classical optimization problem. It is published in a CORE A* journal (Machine Learning) and provides explicit pseudocode (Algorithm 4) that is feasible to implement and reproduce on a standard CPU. The datasets used (RCV1, news20) are publicly available and standard in the field, ensuring no restricted data issues.

valid_students.authors: Chun-Nam John Yu, Thorsten Joachims
valid_students.paper_title: Learning Structural SVMs with Latent Variables
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper for the following reasons:  1. Method Alignment: The paper's primary contribution is Latent Structural SVM, which directly satisfies the SVM/kernel methods requirement.  2. Code Availability: Official C implementation is available from Cornell University (https://www.cs.cornell.edu/~cnyu/latentssvm/) and a Python implementation exists via PyStruct, ensuring strong reproducibility.  3. Venue & Year: Published at ICML 2009, which is an explicitly allowed CORE A* venue within the 2009-2012 timeframe.  4. Reproducibility: The paper demonstrates applications on motif-finding, noun-phrase coreference, and precision@k optimization - all achievable on CPU without heavy compute requirements.  5. Clear Mathematical Formulation: The Concave-Convex Procedure (CCCP) for optimization is well-documented, making implementation and understanding tractable for Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Bing Jian, and Baba C. Vemuri, Fellow
valid_students.paper_title: Robust Point Set Registration Using Gaussian Mixture Models
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it perfectly aligns with the required 2011 classical ML era and CORE A* venue (PAMI). It uses Gaussian Mixture Models, an allowed method, and is highly reproducible because it relies on synthetic 'toy' datasets like 2D shapes. Most importantly, the math can be implemented and run on a standard personal laptop CPU without needing a GPU.

valid_students.authors: Yang Yu, Yu-Feng Li, Zhi-Hua Zhou
valid_students.paper_title: Diversity Regularized Machine
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected “Diversity Regularized Machine” (IJCAI 2011) because it presents a clear and rigorous methodological contribution to classical machine learning, specifically in Support Vector Machines (SVM). The paper introduces a mathematically formulated diversity regularization framework for SVM ensembles and provides both theoretical analysis (PAC-style bounds) and empirical validation. It fits well within the 2009–2012 classical ML era and focuses on core concepts such as margin, hypothesis space complexity, and optimization. Additionally, the method is reproducible on standard UCI datasets using CPU-only implementations, making it suitable for experimental analysis and deeper understanding in subsequent parts of the examination.

valid_students.authors: Jason Lines, Luke M. Davis, Jon Hills, Anthony Bagnall
valid_students.paper_title: A Shapelet Transform for Time Series Classification
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it satisfies all exam constraints: published at KDD 2012 (CORE A*), within the 2010-2012 window, with a clear methodological contribution — the Shapelet Transform algorithm for time series classification. The primary method is classical machine learning (subsequence matching + Information Gain + SVM), with no deep learning involved. The GunPoint dataset from the UCR Time Series Archive is freely available, has 150 consecutive time points, and binary labels. The entire pipeline can be reproduced on a CPU-only laptop using numpy and scikit-learn in under 2 minutes. The core concept — finding discriminative subsequences using Information Gain and transforming time series into distance vectors — is intuitive and explainable without heavy mathematics.

valid_students.authors: Jialu Liu, Deng Cai, Xiaofei He
valid_students.paper_title: Gaussian Mixture Model with Local Consistency
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it lets me deepen my understanding of classical GMMs while also seeing how they can be improved using ideas from manifold learning and local consistency. It stays close to the standard EM framework, so I can clearly see how each modification to the objective and E/M steps affects clustering performance and representation of data structure.

valid_students.authors: Krikamol Muandet, Kenji Fukumizu, Francesco Dinuzzo, Bernhard Schölkopf
valid_students.paper_title: Learning from Distributions via Support Measure Machines
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it explores an advanced and interesting extension of traditional machine learning methods. Instead of learning from individual data points, it focuses on learning from probability distributions, which is a more realistic representation of many real-world problems. I was particularly interested in understanding how kernel methods and SVMs can be generalized using concepts like RKHS and mean embeddings. This paper helped me strengthen my theoretical foundation in machine learning while also exposing me to research-level ideas beyond standard coursework.

valid_students.authors: Michael Mohler, Razvan Bunescu, Rada Mihalcea
valid_students.paper_title: Learning to Grade Short Answer Questions using Semantic Similarity Measures and Dependency Graph Alignments
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it directly aligns with my project on an Automated EdTech Assistant. The paper addresses automatic short answer grading — a core functionality in any intelligent tutoring or edtech system. The primary ML contribution is SVM/SVR, which is used to learn a grading function from multiple text similarity features. The Mohler dataset is publicly available, small enough to run on a CPU-only laptop, and the core model is reproducible using scikit-learn's SVR. The domain connection (student answer evaluation) means I can reason about assumptions, failure cases, and design choices naturally in Parts B and C without forcing artificial connections.

valid_students.authors: Masayuki Karasuyama and Ichiro Takeuchi
valid_students.paper_title: Multiple Incremental Decremental Learning of Support Vector Machines
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this

valid_students.authors: Dengyong Zhou, Lin Xiao, and Mingrui Wu
valid_students.paper_title: Hierarchical Classification via Orthogonal Transfer
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: because its mathematical contribution—modifying the standard SVM loss function with an orthogonality constraint—is very clear. Furthermore, the optimization method (Regularized Dual Averaging) provides a robust algorithmic path for re-implementation in Part B

valid_students.authors: Dan Feldman, Matthew Faulkner, Andreas Krause
valid_students.paper_title: Scalable Training of Mixture Models via Coresets
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: The paper's primary contribution is an efficient sampling algorithm specifically designed to run on CPU-only hardware, making it perfectly reproducible on a standard laptop.

valid_students.authors: Youngmin Cho and Lawrence K. Saul
valid_students.paper_title: Kernel Methods for Deep Learning
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this

valid_students.authors: Yu-Feng Li James T. Kwok Zhi-Hua Zhou 1
valid_students.paper_title: Cost-Sensitive Semi-Supervised Support Vector Machine
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it introduces a clear methodological extension of classical Support Vector Machines by integrating cost-sensitive learning with semi-supervised training. The work is published at AAAI 2010 (CORE A*), falls within the required 2009–2012 timeframe, and makes a genuine algorithmic contribution rather than a simple application.

valid_students.authors: Long Zhu, Yuanhao Chen, Alan Yuille, William Freeman
valid_students.paper_title: Latent Structural SVM for Object Detection
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this

valid_students.authors: Noam Shental, Aharon Bar-Hillel, Tomer Hertz and Daphna Weinshall
valid_students.paper_title: Computing Gaussian Mixture Models with EM using Equivalence Constraints
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it presents a clear methodological extension of Gaussian Mixture Models by incorporating equivalence constraints into the EM algorithm. The contribution is theoretical and algorithmic while remaining computationally feasible for reproduction on a standard CPU-based laptop. The method aligns directly with classical machine learning (GMM and EM), satisfies the 2009–2012 CORE A* venue requirement, and allows experimentation using synthetic Gaussian datasets. The balance between theoretical rigor and practical reproducibility makes it suitable for in-depth analysis in Parts B and C

valid_students.authors: Fabien Lauer FABIEN.LAUER@LORIA.FR Yann Guermeur
valid_students.paper_title: MSVMpack: A Multi-Class Support Vector Machine Package
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it presents a unified and generic formulation of multi-class Support Vector Machines, which is a core classical machine learning method. The paper is methodological in nature and published in JMLR (CORE A*), satisfying the venue and year constraints. It provides a clear optimization framework, dual formulation, and practical implementation details. The experiments are reproducible on CPU-only systems, and simplified versions of the Crammer-Singer M-SVM can be implemented using scikit-learn. The paper also allows analytical study of optimization behavior, scalability with number of classes, and failure modes, making it suitable for deeper conceptual understanding in Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Apoorv Agarwal, Boyi Xie, Ilia Vovsha, Owen Rambow, Rebecca Passonneau
valid_students.paper_title: Sentiment Analysis of Twitter Data
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because I was curious about how machine learning can be used to understand people's emotions from short social media posts like tweets. The paper is easy to follow it clearly explains why Twitter data is challenging and then shows how SVM with smart linguistic features can handle it better than a simple word-counting approach. What I liked most is that the authors don't just throw a model at the problem they actually analyze which features matter and why. The dataset is also publicly available, which means I can actually run experiments and verify the results myself without needing special access or expensive hardware. Overall, it felt like a paper that is both academically solid and practically doable for someone at my level.

valid_students.authors: Jerome Friedman, Trevor Hastie, Robert Tibshirani
valid_students.paper_title: Sparse Inverse Covariance Estimation with the Lasso
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it introduces a computationally efficient method for estimating sparse Gaussian graphical models using L1 regularization. The method, known as the Graphical Lasso, provides a principled way to estimate sparse inverse covariance matrices and identify conditional independence relationships between variables.  The algorithm proposed in the paper is computationally efficient and can be reproduced using standard CPU-based tools such as Python and scikit-learn. The method has clear algorithmic steps and is widely used in machine learning and statistics for graphical model estimation.  This makes the paper suitable for reproduction and experimentation in the exam setting. The algorithm can be implemented using synthetic or publicly available datasets and allows meaningful experiments such as studying the effect of the regularization parameter on sparsity and model structure.

valid_students.authors: Quoc V. Le, Will Y. Zou, and Andrew Y. Ng
valid_students.paper_title: Learning hierarchical invariant spatio-temporal features for action recognition with independent subspace analysis
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it represents a landmark shift in the computer vision pipeline. Traditionally, Support Vector Machines (SVMs) were fed 'hand-crafted' features (like HOG or HOF) that humans designed to detect edges or motion.This paper is significant because it introduces Independent Subspace Analysis (ISA) to automatically learn those features from raw video data. It then uses a Linear or $\chi^2$ SVM as the final classifier to prove that these 'learned' features are more robust and accurate than the features humans spent decades designing. By keeping the SVM constant, the authors isolated the power of their feature-learning method, making it an ideal study for understanding the evolution of Representation Learning in video analysis.

valid_students.authors: Jianxin Wu
valid_students.paper_title: A Fast Dual Method for HIK SVM Learning
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: Gemini said The paper "A Fast Dual Method for HIK SVM Learning" (Jianxin Wu, ECCV 2010) is the best fit for your exam. It covers Unit 2 (SVM), falls within the 2008–2013 window, and was published in an A conference*. It introduces a deterministic Inverse Coordinate Descent (ICD) solver that is faster and easier to implement than standard SVM methods. Crucially for your 48-hour deadline, it is not sensitive to the C parameter, meaning you can skip time-consuming cross-validation and still achieve high accuracy during reproduction.

valid_students.authors: David C. Lee, Abhinav Gupta, Martial Hebert, Takeo Kanade
valid_students.paper_title: Estimating Spatial Layout of Rooms using Volumetric Reasoning about Objects and Surfaces
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I have completely gone through this paper and understood that it represents a highly creative methodological application of Support Vector Machines. Instead of just using SVMs for standard classification, it uses a Structured SVM formulation to solve a complex physical problem: embedding 3D volumetric constraints (spatial exclusion and containment) directly into the ML loss penalty. Furthermore, while the original optimization is complex, the core ideas can be effectively simplified and reproduced using standard modern libraries (like scikit-learn) and approximate inference (beam search) on a CPU, making it an excellent candidate for diagnostic experiments and critical failure analysis in Part B

valid_students.authors: Jesin Zakaria, Abdullah Mueen, Eamonn J. Keogh
valid_students.paper_title: Clustering Time Series Using Unsupervised-Shapelets
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it presents a clear methodological contribution — the unsupervised shapelet (u-shapelet) algorithm — which extends shapelets to work without labeled data for the first time. The core algorithm is based on finding short local subsequences in time series that maximally separate data into groups using a gap metric. The paper uses the UCR Time Series Archive which is freely and publicly available, and the experiments are fully reproducible on a standard CPU-only laptop using Python libraries like tslearn. The paper is from ICDM 2012, a CORE A* venue, and covers a time series method which aligns with my interest. I also verified it is not already selected by any classmate in the response sheet.

valid_students.authors: Saurabh Goorha, Lyle H. Ungar
valid_students.paper_title: Discovery of significant emerging trends
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it applies time-series modeling to the real-world problem of trend detection in streaming data. Since I am comfortable with ARIMA, this paper allows me to build on my existing knowledge by using ARIMA as a baseline to detect 'bursts' or significant deviations in data volume.

valid_students.authors: Yehuda Koren
valid_students.paper_title: Collaborative Filtering with Temporal Dynamics
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it provides a bridge between classical time-series modeling and latent factor models. It addresses the 'concept drift' problem—the reality that user preferences and item popularity are not static but evolve.

valid_students.authors: Kevin Small Byron C. Wallace Carla E. Brodley Thomas A. Trikalinos
valid_students.paper_title: The Constrained Weight Space SVM: Learning with Ranked Features
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected “The Constrained Weight Space SVM: Learning with Ranked Features” (ICML 2011) because it extends the classical Support Vector Machine framework by incorporating domain knowledge directly into the optimization problem through weight constraints. Instead of relying only on labeled instances, the paper allows experts to provide labeled or ranked features, which reduces the amount of training data required. I found this idea interesting because it connects optimization, prior knowledge, and supervised learning in a clean mathematical way. Since the method is based on linear SVMs and standard quadratic programming, it is fully classical, computationally feasible on a CPU, and reproducible using small text or synthetic datasets. The formulation is understandable and allows meaningful experimentation for Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Vikas C. Raykar, Shipeng Yu, Linda H. Zhao, Gerardo Hermosillo Valadez, Charles Florin, Luca Bogoni, Linda Moy
valid_students.paper_title: Learning From Crowds
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it introduces a principled probabilistic framework for learning from multiple noisy annotators using an EM-based latent variable model. The paper presents a clear methodological contribution, including a joint estimation procedure for true labels and annotator reliability. The model is reproducible using synthetic or publicly available datasets and allows controlled experimentation on annotator noise, bias, and class imbalance. Its mathematical clarity and experimental feasibility make it well-suited for detailed reproduction and analysis in subsequent parts of the examination.

valid_students.authors: Rong-En Fan  Kai-Wei Chang  Cho-Jui Hsieh  Xiang-Rui Wang  Chih-Jen Lin
valid_students.paper_title: LIBLINEAR: A Library for Large Linear Classification
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it presents an efficient method for training large-scale linear Support Vector Machine models. The LIBLINEAR library significantly improves training speed and scalability for large datasets while maintaining strong classification performance. The method is widely used in machine learning and can be reproduced using publicly available datasets, making it suitable for further experimentation and implementation.

valid_students.authors: Yehong Zhang   ,Eamonn Keogh , Xiaopeng Yan ,  Chotirat Ann Ratanamahatana , Song Yang
valid_students.paper_title: A Fast Exact Method for Mining Time Series Subsequences under Dynamic Time Warping
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it presents a novel exact algorithm for time series subsequence mining under dynamic time warping, directly aligning with Unit 1 of the syllabus. The methodological contribution is clear and reproducible, with standard datasets that allow for accessible experimentation. The problem formulation and algorithmic design make the paper suitable for both theoretical evaluation and practical implementation required in Parts B, C, and D of the examination.

valid_students.authors: Tianyi Zhou, Dacheng Tao, Xindong Wu
valid_students.paper_title: NESVM: A Fast Gradient Method for Support Vector Machines.
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it presents a clear methodological improvement to SVM training and is feasible to reproduce and analyze within the given constraints.

valid_students.authors: Jun Zhu, Ning Chen, Eric P. Xing
valid_students.paper_title: Infinite SVM: a Dirichlet Process Mixture of Large-margin Kernel Machines
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it introduces a novel Bayesian nonparametric extension of SVM (Infinite SVM) that automatically adapts model complexity while retaining large-margin advantages, and is experimentally reproducible on small, CPU-friendly datasets.

valid_students.authors: Matthieu Rey, Vikas Sindhwani, Guillaume Bouchard
valid_students.paper_title: Copula Mixture Model for Dependency-seeking Clustering
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
