valid_students.authors: Yiming Ying, Colin Campbell, Mark Girolami
valid_students.paper_title: Analysis of SVM with Indefinite Kernels
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it extends classical SVM theory to indefinite kernels, addressing practical cases where kernels are not positive semidefinite. It provides strong theoretical analysis and remains reproducible using modified kernel-based SVM implementations.

valid_students.authors: Tianbao Yang, Yu-Feng Li, Mehrdad Mahdavi, Rong Jin, Zhi-Hua Zhou
valid_students.paper_title: Nyström Method vs Random Fourier Features: A Theoretical and Empirical Comparison
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it directly addresses kernel SVM scalability — a core classical ML topic — by comparing two principled kernel approximation methods: Nyström and Random Fourier Features. The paper is methodologically focused, concise, and has a clear theoretical contribution backed by empirical validation. Both methods are natively implemented in scikit-learn (Nystroem and RBFSampler), making reproduction on a CPU-only laptop straightforward. The datasets used (USPS, GISETTE) are publicly available. The failure conditions are clearly stated and easy to reason about, which will help in Part C.

valid_students.authors: D. Sculley
valid_students.paper_title: Large Scale Learning to Rank
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because in class we mostly worked with SVMs on relatively small datasets (like floor detection), and I was curious about how the same RankSVM idea scales to real-world search engine data with millions of documents. What interested me is how Stochastic Pairwise Descent converts the ranking objective into simple online updates while still preserving the margin intuition of SVMs.  I want to explore how stable the ranking performance remains under noisy preference pairs and compare online updates with traditional batch training. Since the method is CPU-feasible and can be tested on synthetic or LETOR-style datasets, it gives me room to both implement and critically analyze the model.

valid_students.authors: Weilong Yang, Yang Wang, Arash Vahdat, Greg Mori
valid_students.paper_title: Kernel Latent SVM for Visual Recognition
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: 1. similar to what we have studied in tha class (finding the walkable path/floor in the image).  2. eazy to implement.  3. dataset available publicly.  4. Clear real-world application: object detection.  5. Core method is classical machine learning (SVM variant).

valid_students.authors: Cho-Jui Hsieh , Kai-Wei Chang , Chih-Jen Lin
valid_students.paper_title: Dual Coordinate Descent Methods for Linear SVM
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it proposes a clear and efficient methodological improvement for training linear Support Vector Machines using dual coordinate descent. The contribution is purely methodological, well-defined mathematically, and reproducible on a standard CPU without requiring special datasets or heavy computation. The algorithm is practical, scalable, and implemented in widely used libraries, making it feasible to reproduce and analyze within the scope of this examination.

valid_students.authors: Suicheng Gu and Yuhong Guo
valid_students.paper_title: Learning SVM Classifiers with Indefinite Kernels
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it presents a methodological extension of classical Support Vector Machines to handle indefinite kernels. The work directly modifies the SVM optimization framework, aligning clearly with classical kernel methods. The experiments can be simplified and reproduced on CPU-only systems using synthetic or small public datasets, making it feasible for deeper implementation and analysis in subsequent parts.

valid_students.authors: Koby Crammer, Alex Kulesza, Mark Dredze
valid_students.paper_title: Adaptive Regularization of Weight Vectors
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose "Adaptive Regularization of Weight Vectors" (AROW) because it represents a significant methodological advancement in online margin-based learning. While standard SVMs focus on finding a fixed margin, AROW maintains a distribution over weight vectors, allowing it to adaptively regularize updates based on confidence.

valid_students.authors: Xiaodong Wang, Wenliang Chen, and Yuhong Yang
valid_students.paper_title: On the Consistency of ARMA Model Selection
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected “On the Consistency of ARMA Model Selection” (JMLR 2011) because it provides a rigorous methodological analysis of model order selection within the ARMA framework, which is a fundamental component of ARIMA time-series modeling. Since ARMA is a special case of ARIMA (with differencing parameter d = 0), the paper directly aligns with the ARIMA/time-series requirement of the examination. The core contribution is methodological, focusing on the theoretical behavior of model selection criteria such as AIC and BIC under different asymptotic conditions.  The paper does not rely on proprietary or restricted datasets and can be reproduced using synthetic ARMA-generated time series on a standard CPU-only setup. This makes it fully compliant with the reproducibility constraints of the exam. Additionally, the theoretical nature of the work allows for deep analytical reasoning regarding overfitting, underfitting, and consistency, which will be valuable for Parts B and C of the examination.

valid_students.authors: John Langford, Lihong Li, Tong Zhang
valid_students.paper_title: Sparse Online Learning via Truncated Gradient
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it introduces a methodological contribution in sparse online learning through truncated gradient optimization. The approach is aligned with classical SVM-style linear models and is computationally feasible on a CPU-only setup. The experiments can be reproduced using publicly available datasets or synthetic data. The paper clearly explains the optimization objective, sparsity mechanism, and failure cases, making it suitable for analytical exploration in Parts B and C.

valid_students.authors: Justin Ma, Lawrence K. Saul, Stefan Savage, Geoffrey M. Voelker
valid_students.paper_title: Beyond Blacklists: Learning to Detect Malicious Web Sites from Suspicious URLs
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it applies SVM to a clear, real-world problem — detecting malicious (phishing/malware) websites using only features extracted from URLs. The primary method is classical SVM with both linear and RBF kernels, published at KDD 2009 (CORE A*). The dataset is publicly available on the UCI Machine Learning Repository under a CC BY 4.0 license, requiring no special access or approval. The entire experimental pipeline — loading data, training an SVM classifier, and evaluating accuracy/precision/recall — can be reproduced on a standard CPU-only laptop using scikit-learn in under 50 lines of Python. The paper's contribution is application-driven and methodological, not just a dataset or benchmark.

valid_students.authors: John P. Cunningham , Zoubin Ghahramani , Carl E. Rasmussen
valid_students.paper_title: Gaussian Processes for time-marked time-series data
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: Its Interesting how time-series data basically discrete events can be modelled as continuous function using Gaussian Processes

valid_students.authors: Kai Zhang,James T. Kwok,Bahram Parvin
valid_students.paper_title: Prototype Vector Machine for Large Scale Semi-Supervised Learning
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it proposes a scalable extension of SVM-based semi-supervised learning using prototype selection to reduce computational cost. It is methodologically focused, aligned with core ML concepts, and reproducible using public datasets on standard CPU hardware.

valid_students.authors: Chih-Wei Hsieh, Kai-Wei Chang, Chih-Jen Lin
valid_students.paper_title: A Dual Coordinate Descent Method for Large-scale Linear SVM
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose “A Dual Coordinate Descent Method for Large-scale Linear SVM” because it perfectly aligns with the requirements of Part A. The paper presents a methodological contribution to classical machine learning by proposing an efficient dual coordinate descent algorithm for large-scale linear SVMs, which can be reproduced on small datasets using a standard CPU-only setup.  The experiments in the paper are straightforward, use public datasets such as RCV1 and MNIST, and can be simplified for student-level reproduction. The dual coordinate descent method is well-explained, and its convergence, update rules, and hyperparameter impact are clearly described.  This paper is ideal for deep understanding of SVM optimization, allows hands-on implementation, and provides clear opportunities to explore failure cases and parameter tuning, which will be tested in Parts B and C. Additionally, it is published in a CORE A venue (JMLR 2011)* and fully satisfies all mandatory selection criteria, ensuring that the paper is valid, reproducible, and methodologically appropriate.

valid_students.authors: Nino Shervashidze, Pascal Schweitzer, Erik Jan van Leeuwen, Kurt Mehlhorn, Karsten M. Borgwardt
valid_students.paper_title: Weisfeiler-Lehman Graph Kernels
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I've always been drawn to problems where machine learning steps outside its usual territory and helps solve real-world scientific questions. This paper caught my attention because it addresses a genuinely fascinating problem — predicting whether a chemical compound will cause DNA mutations — by representing molecules as graphs (atoms as nodes, bonds as edges) and defining a clever kernel to measure structural similarity between them. What excites me is the creative bridge it builds: borrowing a 1968 graph isomorphism algorithm from pure mathematics and repurposing it as a feature extraction scheme for SVMs, turning a theoretical tool into a practical molecular classification pipeline. As someone who enjoys learning across different fields, I wanted a paper where I could understand kernel methods and SVMs deeply while also picking up something new about computational chemistry and molecular biology. The paper is also very implementation-friendly — the WL algorithm is clean, runs in linear time, uses standard SVM classification, and all benchmark datasets (MUTAG, NCI1, ENZYMES) are publicly available. I'm sure I can reproduce the key experiments on a laptop, and explain the design decisions in the proctored exam.

valid_students.authors: Gautam Kunapuli Richard Maclin Jude W. Shavlik
valid_students.paper_title: Advice Refinement in Knowledge-Based SVMs
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it introduces a unique way to combine human expert advice with classical Support Vector Machines (SVM). It follows all exam rules: it is from a CORE A venue , published in 2011, and focuses on a methodological contribution. Most importantly, it is CPU-friendly and can be easily reproduced on a standard laptop using simple, public datasets.

valid_students.authors: Zenglin Xu, Rong Jin, Haiqin Yang, Irwin King, Michael R. Lyu
valid_students.paper_title: Simple and Efficient Multiple Kernel Learning by Group Lasso
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it proposes a simple and efficient optimization method for Multiple Kernel Learning by linking it with group lasso regularization, which directly improves SVM and kernel-based learning. The contribution is clearly methodological rather than application-based, and it was published in ICML 2010, a CORE A* conference, so it satisfies the venue and year constraints. Additionally, the approach is mathematically clear and can be implemented using standard optimization techniques on a CPU, making it practical to reproduce and suitable for deeper understanding in this exam.

valid_students.authors: Stefano Melacci, Mikhail Belkin
valid_students.paper_title: Laplacian Support Vector Machines Trained in the Primal
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it proposes an improved method for training Support Vector Machines using Laplacian regularization in the primal form. The paper is published in JMLR (a CORE A* venue) and falls within the allowed time period. The method is based on classical machine learning (SVM) and can be reproduced on a standard laptop using small datasets. The algorithm also allows simplified experimentation and helps understand semi-supervised learning and graph-based regularization concepts.

valid_students.authors: Dilan Görür,  Carl Edward Rasmussen
valid_students.paper_title: Dirichlet Process Gaussian Mixture Models
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I selected this paper because it introduces a classical Bayesian extension of Gaussian Mixture Models that automatically determines the number of clusters in data. The method is conceptually clear, computationally feasible on a standard CPU laptop, and can be reproduced using small public datasets or synthetic data. The algorithm is also compatible with standard Python libraries such as scikit-learn, which makes experimentation and analysis practical within the constraints of the course examination.

valid_students.authors: Ali Rahimi, Benjamin Recht
valid_students.paper_title: Scalable Support Vector Machines using Random Fourier Features
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose this paper because it works on improving the efficiency of Support Vector Machines for large datasets. The idea of approximating kernel functions using random Fourier features makes the training process faster while still keeping good performance. I found this approach interesting because it connects theoretical concepts with practical machine learning implementation. The method can also be reproduced using publicly available datasets and standard libraries, which makes it suitable for experimenting and analyzing the results in the later parts of the assignment.

valid_students.authors: Abdullah Mueen, Eamonn Keogh
valid_students.paper_title: Online discovery and maintenance of time series motifs
valid_students.understands_responsibility: Yes, I understand and accept this
valid_students.why_chosen: I chose the paper “Online Discovery and Maintenance of Time Series Motifs” (Mueen & Keogh, KDD 2010) because it fits all the requirements of the assignment while also being a problem I personally find intuitive and interesting to work with. The paper is published in KDD 2010, which lies within the 2009–2012 window and is a CORE A* venue listed in the instructions. Its main contribution is a methodological algorithm for time-series motif discovery, which directly aligns with the time series modeling category allowed in the guidelines.  I was particularly drawn to this problem because the idea of finding repeating patterns in time-series data feels very natural and relatable. Many real-world signals—such as heart rate data, sensor readings, stock prices, or activity patterns—contain repeating behaviors, and discovering these patterns automatically is a meaningful machine learning task. The paper focuses on doing this in real time as new data arrives, which makes the problem even more interesting because it reflects how many real systems actually operate.  Another reason I felt confident choosing this paper is that the algorithm is conceptually clear and feasible to implement on a standard laptop using simple tools like Python and NumPy. Since the core ideas involve sliding windows, normalization, and distance comparisons between subsequences, I believe I can understand the method deeply, reproduce the experiments, and clearly explain my reasoning in later parts of the exam. This makes the paper a good balance between being academically meaningful and practically manageable for me to study and reproduce.
